algorithms.name: 52-Week High Proximity
algorithms.reason: George & Hwang (2004, JoF): assets trading closest to their 52-week trailing high earn superior next-month returns due to investor anchoring — participants under-react to positive news when price approaches the reference point, creating a persistent drift. Distinct from past-return momentum; internationally documented across 49 markets.

algorithms.name: Copy insider buys (Form 4, S&P 500)
algorithms.reason: Mirror disclosed open-market insider PURCHASES (SEC Form 4, code P) among liquid S&P 500 names, held ~3 months from the filing date. Buy-side insider trades carry a documented out-of-sample premium (Lakonishok-Lee; Cohen-Malloy-Pomorski); sells are ignored as noisy. Disclosure-date keyed, one parameter (hold horizon), no discretion.

algorithms.name: Copy insider buys — cluster-sell exit
algorithms.reason: Equal-weight insider buy mirror PLUS an early exit when >= 2 insiders sell a held name (a disclosed cluster sell). Insider sells are mostly noise (taxes/10b5-1), so only broad independent selling triggers an exit; lone sales are ignored. One knob, same universe/hold/disclosure discipline as the baseline it must beat.

algorithms.name: Copy insider buys — conviction-weighted
algorithms.reason: Weight each disclosed open-market insider buy by cluster_size x ln(1+$value): multiple independent insider buys (clusters) are the strongest documented insider signal (Lakonishok-Lee; Cohen-Malloy-Pomorski), and dollar size separates real conviction from token purchases. Log damping is standard, not tuned. Same universe, hold horizon, disclosure-date discipline as the equal-weight baseline it must beat.

algorithms.name: Gold trend (Donchian 50/25 GLD)
algorithms.reason: Time-series trend following on gold — a century-scale documented edge (Moskowitz-Ooi-Pedersen 2012; Hurst-Ooi-Pedersen 2017) on the book's first non-equity, low-correlation sleeve. Reuses the incumbent Donchian 50/25 breakout with ZERO parameters tuned on gold data. GLD is physically backed (no futures roll drag), so unlike oil wrappers the ETF's trend is the underlying's trend.

algorithms.name: Low-Vol Anomaly top-3
algorithms.reason: Frazzini & Pedersen (2014) 'Betting Against Beta': leverage-constrained and benchmark-aware investors overpay for high-volatility assets, so the lowest-realized-vol names earn superior risk-adjusted returns out-of-sample across 21+ country samples (Baker & Haugen 2012; Blitz & van Vliet 2007). Select the 3 least-volatile ETFs, size inversely to realized vol (risk parity within the selection), rebalance monthly.

algorithms.name: Regime Switch (trend/vol router)
algorithms.reason: Trend rules earn in sustained moves; mean reversion earns in chop. Route between them with the best-documented state variables: the 200-day trend filter (Faber 2007, JWM) and realized-volatility clustering (Engle 1982; Schwert 1989). Risk-on (SPY above its 200d MA and 20d vol under 30% annualized) holds 1/2 DualMomentum + 1/2 Donchian; risk-off holds MeanReversion, which in stress is naturally mostly cash. All three sub-rules already have measured records in this tournament — this adds only the routing layer. Null hypothesis: the equal-weight ensemble of the same three rules; if switching cannot beat that forward, it is noise.

algorithms.name: Residual Momentum 12mo
algorithms.reason: Blitz, Huij & Martens (2011, JFQA) 'Residual Momentum': subtract the market beta component from each asset's trailing 12-month return to isolate the idiosyncratic underreaction signal. Daniel & Moskowitz (2016, JFE) show that raw momentum crashes are driven by its market-factor loading — a residual signal sidesteps this while preserving the alpha. Rank sector ETFs by residual return, hold the top 3, rebalance monthly.

algorithms.name: Risk Parity (All Weather, unlevered)
algorithms.reason: Bridgewater All Weather (Dalio, 1996): a 60/40 book holds 60% of its dollars in equities but ~90% of its RISK there, so it is not actually diversified. Weight inversely to volatility instead, equalising each asset's risk contribution. The mechanism is leverage aversion (Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen 2012, FAJ, 'Leverage Aversion and Risk Parity'): investors who cannot borrow bid up high-beta assets, leaving low-beta assets better risk-adjusted returns; supporting evidence in Chaves et al. (2011) and Clarke et al. (2013). This is the UNLEVERED variant because the project forbids leverage, so judge it on Sharpe and drawdown, not total return. One conventional parameter (60-day vol).

algorithms.name: TSMOM vol-weighted 12mo
algorithms.reason: Absolute (time-series) momentum, the durable Moskowitz-Ooi-Pedersen effect: hold a name only while its own trailing 12-month return is positive, size inversely to its volatility (risk parity within the long book), rebalance monthly to keep turnover — and thus cost — low. Distinct from the cross-sectional DualMomentum already tested.

algorithms.name: Vol-Targeted Equal Weight
algorithms.reason: Moreira & Muir (2017, JoF 72(4)) 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios': scale equity exposure inversely to recent realized variance; the conditional equity Sharpe ratio is counter-cyclical (highest in calm markets, near-zero or negative during spikes), so a static exposure strategy wastes its risk budget at the worst moments. Hold all instruments equally but reduce total exposure when portfolio vol exceeds a 10% annual target and increase it when vol is low — capped at 1× (long-only, no leverage).

note: Both researchers are propose-only. An algorithm reaches the backtest tournament, and a stock the basket, only after a human approves it in Discord — the LLM never trades and never promotes a proposal to forward/paper/live on its own.
